back: Datasets so large, fast-moving, and varied that traditional single-machine tools can't handle them.
Volume (how much), velocity (how fast it arrives), and variety (how many forms) — the defining traits of big data.
Spreading data and work across many cheap machines that act as one team, instead of relying on a single big machine.
Google's technique (2004) for processing huge datasets in parallel across many machines; powerful but slow and low-level.
The open-source framework (2006) that brought distributed storage (HDFS) and processing (MapReduce) to everyone.
Cheap, near-limitless, pay-as-you-go storage (e.g. Amazon S3) that separates storage from compute.
A single cheap repository for all raw data in any format — store first, structure later.
Applying structure to data only when you read it, not when you write it. Flexible, the opposite of schema-on-write.
A data lake that decayed into a messy, untrustworthy mess because it had no rules or reliability guarantees.
Lakes gained cheap storage and flexibility but lost the reliability and easy querying that warehouses provided.
